data.title: COPX: Copper Could Become AI Gold
data.description:
Thesis

One of my better picks in recent days has been Fluor Corporation, an engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) company that specializes in advanced projects, like setting up pharmaceutical manufacturing. Fluor also constructs data centers, although it doesn’t rank among the biggest players in that field yet. As far as I have found so far, most of the leaders in data center construction are privately held. Still, amid the AI boom, we can expect data center construction to expand and enjoy tailwinds. This, in turn, should provide tailwinds for crucial data center materials, including copper.

Data centers alone will provide considerable tailwinds. That said, we must be careful not to overestimate the impact. Currently, data centers account for only about 2% of copper consumption, but analysts predict that by 2050 they may consume as much as 9%. By itself, 9% may not move the needle massively. However, electric vehicles consume 3X as much copper as internal combustion engine vehicles and EV sales are rising globally.

I also don’t think we can rule out data centers gobbling up even more copper than expected. China is still playing catch-up in the AI race, but I doubt they will concede. Indeed, I believe there’s a good chance that China will adopt an even more proactive approach in building AI centers, which could drive demand for copper.

In light of all of the above, I rate the Global X Copper Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:COPX) a buy but suggest keeping investments moderate.

Data Centers Consume Vast Quantities Of Copper

Copper is one of the best conductors of electricity. In power lines and large-scale energy grids, aluminum is often favored because it is substantially lighter, among other things. Yet when it comes to smaller facilities, copper is often king. With data centers being built out the world over, vast amounts of copper will be needed. One study found that every megawatt of applied power at a data center will require roughly 27 tonnes of copper. Currently, AI data centers equal to or greater than 200MW are becoming common, which should consume more than 5,000 tonnes of copper.

Further, data centers can’t simply be thrown out into a field and must obviously be connected to some sort of power source. When local grid networks need to be expanded, demand for copper should increase. While aluminum is favored in many grid applications, copper demand should still receive a boost. Indeed, in 2025, about 12.52 million tons of copper were used in grid infrastructure, and consumption is projected to hit 14.87 million metric tons by 2030. Many companies building data centers are also looking at separate, off-grid power solutions, including nuclear power, solar panels, and wind turbines. If power sources are placed near data centers, copper may be favored owing to its better conductivity.

Whatever direction is taken, expanding the energy grid is looking crucial, with industry experts warning of bottlenecks. I expect these constraints to result in substantial public and private investments. Renewables may enjoy a boost as well, and wind turbines, solar panels, and other technologies often use high levels of copper.

Watch For China’s AI Investments To Ramp Up

Then there’s China. American curbs on chip exports, including exports abroad that use American technologies and IP, have slowed China’s efforts in the AI game. However, China is getting increasingly aggressive in leveraging rare earths and other forms of leverage to pressure the United States. Given China’s chokehold over rare earths, which are crucial for defense and technology supply chains and production, I believe the United States will be forced to offer serious concessions, and chips will likely be a major factor. This could produce a flood of semiconductor orders and data center build-outs.

Interestingly enough, however, efforts to shift away from American chips are gaining steam. The Chinese Communist Party (CCP) has banned major tech companies from using Nvidia chips and instead is pushing for the development of homegrown solutions. China isn’t going to catch up overnight, but I expect it to focus on quantity over quality for right now. China’s processors may not be able to go toe to toe any time soon with the best American chips, but they may be able to close the gap by building bigger data centers.

Crucially, China’s energy grid is believed to have substantially more capacity to support AI and data centers. This may reduce the need to prioritize energy efficiency and may allow China to simply outbuild the United States.

EVs Need Copper Too

As already mentioned, EVs use 3X more copper than an ICE vehicle. Electric vehicles now account for the majority of new car sales in China. In Europe, EV auto sales are up ~25% YOY (first half). On a global scale, EV sales are up nearly 30%. With Chinese EV companies like BYD rapidly expanding globally, I suspect that electric vehicles will gain ground in many other countries. Budget-friendly Chinese autos could make car ownership more attainable in emerging markets, thus fueling building growth.

Global X Copper Miners ETF Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

First, I believe that geographical diversification is prudent. Over 90% of COPX’s equity holdings are non-American assets, according to Morningstar. Many companies also maintain global footprints outside of where they are headquartered. So while COPX may not intentionally position itself as a foreign ETF, it does offer a lot of overseas exposure. I find this attractive, especially amid global trade tensions. I believe a well-diversified portfolio will offer (relatively) lower risks and higher upside should the world start to decouple.

With ETFs, I often look at individual valuation and fundamental considerations of major holdings. However, none of the 40 equity holdings top 6% of weight (the highest is 5.5%). With that in mind, I believe it’s crucial to step back to look at the portfolio-wide numbers. COPX’s PE ratio is a rather enticing 16.88, a bit below the category median of 17.64 (Morningstar). This ratio strikes me as rather favorable, given that copper could enjoy solid demand growth in the years ahead. The Price/Sales ratio is also quite attractive at 0.97 (ibid.). Commodities often do carry lower PS ratios but this is still well below the category average of 1.39 (ibid.). Cash flow growth is moderate at 1.08%, but that’s far better than the -6.55 category average (ibid.).

COPX has a pretty high adjusted expense ratio at .65% (Morningstar) but this in line with the .65% expenses for the Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL, Seeking Alpha). The Sprott Copper Miners ETF (COPP) also has a .65% expense ratio (Seeking Alpha). Risks for these ETFs are currently graded as a D- by Seeking Alpha. With COPX there is significant short-selling activity at 8.40%, compared to 4.69% short-selling for SIL (Seeking Alpha). This gap doesn't worry me as silver has been a hot commodity to hedge against inflation, similar to gold.

Still, COPX is a relatively risky investment. Tracking errors are especially a sore spot, with the 3-year at 26.23%, which is actually much better than SIL's 34.98%. COPP is a bit better on this front at 23.95%, but this ETF has only been on the market for about a year, so I'll go with the longer-established ETF. COPP is also more highly concentrated with the top 10 holdings accounting for more than 70% of the portfolio. SIL is even more heavily concentrated with the top 10 holdings accounting for more than 75%. With this type of play based on broad tailwinds, I prefer high diversification. Ultimately, I believe upside justifies the risk for COPX, but would keep investments moderate.

The dividend yield (TTM) is only 1.29% and growth rates are all over the place. The TTM growth rate surged to nearly 42%, but the 3-year CAGR is nearly 11% in the red. Still, the fund has paid dividends for 14 straight years, well above the category average of 4 years. Unfortunately, however, this is a rather expensive ETF in terms of fees, with the adjusted expense ratio a rather hefty 0.650%. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools ding the expenses with rather mediocre marks, but overall, like me, the tools give a buy rating. SIL, for comparison has a dividend yield of just 1.12% (Seeking Alpha).

Why I Could Be Wrong

If the economy goes into recession, construction projects will likely slow at least temporarily, which could deflate COPX’s price. Construction is copper-intensive, and construction tends to be rather cyclical. Meanwhile, should the AI bubble pop, the data center thesis will probably go out the window (at least for a time). I do think AI is getting bubbly, but I don’t think it’ll pop any time soon. While electricity is a rather mature technology, I don’t think we can fully write off technological breakthroughs either. Copper was once pretty popular for transferring data, for example, but is being phased out in favor of fiber optics in key areas.

Key Takeaway: COPX Looks Like A Buy

All-in-all, I believe COPX will enjoy AI-related tailwinds. Add in energy grid upgrades and electric vehicles, and there are enough tailwinds here for me to give COPX a buy rating. That said, the tracking errors are a sore spot, even if they are somewhat common with commodity ETFs. I would keep investments moderate. I view this as a moderate risk investment with good upside.

data.title: Check Point Software: Growth Awaits Management's Drive To Push Its Go-To-Market Strategy
data.description:
Check Point Software Technologies (NASDAQ:CHKP) will be reporting q3’25 earnings on October 28, 2025 before market open. Analysts are relatively mixed on expectations, showing 18x EPS revisions up and 14x down in the last 90 days. There are a number of factors that I believe will determine investors’ reactions following the earnings release, including updates on Check Point’s AI strategy, its go-to-market strategy, and further discounting of its platform services. Given the strong opportunity for AI security and Check Point’s engagement in building its arsenal of AI security applications, I am recommending CHKP shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $198/share at 17.10x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Check Point Software Technologies Operations

Check Point operates under 4 core principles:

  1. Securing the connectivity fabric
  2. Prevention-first ethos
  3. Open platform philosophy
  4. AI-first security

Looking at eq3’25, investors will be looking for growth in billings as Check Point aims to accelerate growth. Management has indicated a focus in advancing Check Point’s go-to-market strategy, which may not be apparent in the historical financials given the flat sequential sales & marketing operating expense in q2’25.

The other driver for growing billings will be new product offerings across email, ERM, and SASE, as well as the unified platform. With the notion of growing the top line by reinvesting in the organization, Check Point may experience modest margin compression in the near term before realizing stronger operating leverage.

One driving factor going into eq3’25 earnings will be broad adoption of Check Point’s AI-powered security features as enterprise adoption of AI becomes the new threat vector. I believe securing an enterprise environment for AI will largely involve a functional Zero Trust platform, the core of tracking identities, both human and machine. In relation to this, Check Point was recognized as a leader for its Zero Trust Platform by Forrester Wave on July 11, 2025 including its coverage across the network, cloud, and endpoint controls for hybrid environments.

Zero Trust has been an area of interest as enterprises adopt agentic AI and GenAI to automate various administrative tasks. The Zero Trust platform is expected to become one of the prominent security features for an enterprise, particularly as enterprises adopt agentic AI to augment personnel. The basis behind this is that AI agents will have access to critical, sensitive data for autonomous use, exposing enterprises to potential threats of agent injection into an environment.

Enhancing its platform, Check Point acquired Veriti in May 2025 to provide AI-driven, real-time threat intelligence enforcement, automated patching, and contextual remediation of security threats. Veriti monitors logs, identifies vulnerabilities, and engages in threat prevention in real time, reducing remediation time and the need for human intervention.

More recently, Check Point announced the acquisition of Lakera on September 16, 2025. Lakera is an AI-native, end-to-end AI security stack for securing agentic AI applications, building on Check Point’s security offerings to protect enterprises during the full lifecycle of AI development and utilization. Lakera’s security solutions provide advanced runtime protection with continuous threat hunting, creating an active security posture for an enterprise. In addition to this, Lakera is powered by Gandalf, a continuous threat intelligence feature that helps detect adversarial patterns.

One feature Check Point is bringing forth is its pay-as-you-go offerings to provide more financial flexibility to customers. As opposed to paying for a subscription upfront, customers can spread the financial burden over time, incentivizing them to utilize more features across the security platform. This was a core transformation for CrowdStrike (CRWD) with its Falcon Flex platform.

Check Point Software Technologies Financial Position

Looking to eq3’25, I’m forecasting Check Point to generate $675mm in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.44/share. One of the factors that I will be looking for in the upcoming earnings release is management’s commitment to bolstering its go-to-market initiative to drive future revenue growth. Though reinvesting in sales & marketing may compress margins in the near-term, I believe Check Point can realize improved operating leverage over time as it expands its sales initiative.

Looking at Check Point for the long run, I believe e2h25 will set the tone for future growth with management’s commitment to enhancing the firm’s go-to-market strategy. Depending on the strategy, Check Point may have a stronger runway going into the outer years, particularly as the firm competes with firms like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) for multi-nodal AI security.

Risks Related to Check Point Software Technologies

Bull Case

Check Point has a strong market for growth as enterprise AI adoption takes place, potentially adding the need to enhance cybersecurity posture for zero trust. I believe that could realize a strong growth trajectory as it reinvests in expanding its AI capabilities, both for security operations and security posture, though the firm needs to show analysts that it remains committed to bolstering its sales & marketing strategy.

Bear Case

Check Point is operating in a highly competitive market for AI security, potentially posing a threat to its future growth trajectory. Check Point indicated significant discounting to drive platform sales, potentially suggesting that the firm may be at risk of losing market share to other vendors.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Check Point repurchased 1.5mm shares at an average price of $220/share in q2’25, summing to roughly $330mm. Check Point has historically utilized share repurchases to enhance shareholder value; I believe Check Point may continue repurchasing shares going into e2h25 to bolster its earnings per share [EPS].

CHKP shares currently trade at 17.58x EV/aEBITDA, just above the midpoint of its 1-year historical trading range. Taking into consideration its peer comps, CHKP shares are trading at a relative discount, potentially providing room for a higher premium if the firm exhibits improved growth.

Valuing CHKP shares, I believe shares should be priced at $198/share at 17.10x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending CHKP shares with a Buy rating.

We can come up with a similar price range given the low volatility & risk exhibited in CHKP shares. Compared to peer cybersecurity stocks, CHKP has a relatively low 24-month beta of 0.49x, potentially offering a more value-oriented approach to investing in the sector.

Multiples Model Description

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative marketcap and relative multiple.

DCF Model Description

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.

data.title: Lavoro Cannot Cover Its Cost Of Capital, And The Situation Is Too Hairy
data.description:
Lavoro (NASDAQ:LVRO) is an agchem retailer and manufacturer, operating primarily in Brazil, but also in Colombia and Uruguay.

The company's financial position is critical, as it has not filed financials for the 1H25 calendar operations, has required supplier renegotiation agreements, and was posting large losses in 2H24 calendar (1H25 fiscal). This was generated by large interest expenses on its working capital, added to only operating breakeven profitability since the agchem cycle turned negative in Brazil.

Given the context, I believe the company will need more than it has announced to sustain profitable operations, generating a risk of dilution. In addition, the situation is unclear given the lack of recent financials. For that reason, I initiate Lavoro with a Hold rating.

Brazil and LatAm agchem retailer

Lavoro is a large agchemical retailer and distributor, operating primarily in Brazil.

According to the company's latest 20-F, for FY24 (ended June 2024), the company generates 86% of its revenues in Brazil, with a store network of about 190 stores, and about a thousand sellers. The company also operates retail operations in Colombia and Uruguay, for the remaining 14% of revenues.

In addition, the company has a small manufacturing segment (called Crop Care) that manufactures or imports non-patented products for its retail operations. The segment generated 10% of the company's revenues (mixed between Brazil and other countries).

The company is large in the Brazilian market, but not dominant. According to the 20-F as well (under Brazilian Major Agricultural Input Lines), the Brazilian agchem market was worth R$360 billion, or about $65 billion. Lavoro generated revenues of $1.6 billion in FY24, so a 2.5% market share, approximately. Not small, but obviously not a price setter.

Terrible cycle in agriculture

The company is not an agchem company per se, but rather a commercializer of those products. It is clearly affected by the agchem cycle, but not in the same way as companies that do full production or R&D.

Still, Lavoro has suffered from a very negative cycle in agriculture after prices fell post-2022, which has led to low farm margins and low investment in agchem products. The cycle was worsened by the fact that crop prices went down a lot more than oil prices, which are a driver of agchem prices.

The cycle affected Lavoro in several ways. Its inventory turnover decreased, increasing the cost of carrying working capital (a fundamental factor in its financial situation, as we will see below). Its margins decreased because inventories bought at higher prices had to be sold at a loss or written down, and in general, the volume of business became too small to sustain its SG&A structure.

Hard to repay the cost of working capital

As we can see above, as of the latest complete financials (September 2024, or 1Q25, more on this later), the company was operationally breakeven.

However, its net losses were very large, up to R$200 million (~$40 million) in 1Q25 alone, because of the interest costs it cannot cover.

The company does not have a lot of debt per se, with 'only' R$1.2 billion in borrowings in 1Q25, versus more than R$500 million in cash. However, the company has to pay interest on its working capital financing, basically its payables to suppliers. This makes up the majority of the company's interest expenses in FY24 and in 1Q25.

The reason is that the cost of capital is not cheap in Brazil. The interbank secured overnight rate (called SELIC) is 15%, meaning a supplier 'lending' inventory to Lavoro is 'losing' the opportunity to earn interest on the money if it charged for the sale upfront.

Indeed, the cost of R$115 million for one quarter of payables (R$600 million annualized) yields an interest rate of about 14% when compared to R$4.2 billion in trade payables in 1Q25.

Financial tightness and restructuring

The company's balance sheet up to 1Q25 was not that bad, given that the company had cash for about R$500 million, and current assets matched current liabilities.

It is true that cash flows were not positive, especially in 1Q25, with a negative R$735 million operationally. However, agrichem companies in Brazil or the Southern Cone tend to build working capital in the first half of the fiscal year (second half of the calendar year) because of the seasonality of crop planting.

For FY24 and FY23 (20-F page 209), operating cash flows had been positive, by R$165 and R$108 million, respectively, inclusive of interest expenses, albeit with a big buildup of payables (R$1 billion higher financing from payables in FY24).

However, in late 2024, the financial situation in the agricultural value chain in Brazil started to get hairy. One of Lavoro's competitors, Agrogalaxy, requested financial bankruptcy in September 2024 (exactly 1Q25, the last complete report from Lavoro), and Brazilian farm loan delinquencies started to surge.

In this context, Lavoro's suppliers started to tighten their conditions and decided not to give more credit to the company. Lavoro stopped filing financials for a long time, until it announced a supplier restructuring agreement in June 2025.

In the release, the company explained that 2Q25 (ended December 2024) had been terrible, with sales falling around 30% in Brazil, and gross profits even more, implying large operational losses (the operating income figure, or income statement, were not provided), because it had no inventory to sell, as suppliers tightened their credit.

The agreement includes the renegotiation of about R$2.5 billion in payables (about 60% of the company's payables in 1Q25), which will be repaid in installments over between 5 and 10 years, indexed to inflation. The company clarified that the suppliers would also continue to provide credit (in the form of merchandise) to the company, but did not comment on how operations had proceeded in the meantime (2H25 fiscal, 1H25 calendar).

In addition, there were no comments about potential benefits to the suppliers (like equity or an interest rate). The conditions reported (inflation-indexing) imply a significant haircut to suppliers (again, imagine that they could earn 15% on the money, versus 4/5% from inflation-indexing).

The situation remains too risky

Again, Lavoro has not filed complete financials for 2Q25 (we don't know how much it lost from operations), nor for the 2H25 fiscal period, all of which also happened without an agreement with suppliers.

Losses could be very large, assuming a 30% haircut to revenues for the whole fiscal year, and flat SG&A, we could be talking of operational losses of as much as $75 million: $240 million in gross profits in FY24 by 70% becomes $175 million, minus $250 million in SG&A is $75 million in operating losses.

We do not know the new shape of the balance sheet, how much cash is left, or the damage to customer relations from not being able to serve them. The potential operational loss above would almost wipe out the R$500 million (less than $100 million) in cash the company had.

Only from this perspective, the company is simply too risky for investment. It would be basically like buying a lottery ticket without knowing the payouts or the probabilities.

Further, the company will need massive capitalization even beyond the payables restructuring. During the best period in its reported history, the company generated about $65 million in operating profitability. This was during the peak cycle for crops, during the post-pandemic + Ukraine war boom.

At that time, the company was already unable to cover its financial structure. That simply means the company is unable to cover its cost of capital if it comes from debt (its return on assets is below the cost of financing).

It would not face this problem with equity, which has only a residual claim, although it is possible that equity financers would simply earn a low return.

That is, even assuming an instantaneous recovery to the best position of the cycle, the company would still be unprofitable without financing. This implies high risks for equity, either from bankruptcy (a restructuring with suppliers is very close to that) or from dilution.

For those reasons, I initiate Lavoro with a Hold rating, waiting for more news.

data.title: TAL Education: Turning Positive On Potential Q2 Beat And Generous Capital Returns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become bullish on TAL Education Group (NYSE:TAL).

The analysts' conservative financial projections are setting up the stage for a 2QFY2026 (YE end-Feb '26) beat. Concerns about potential cash hoarding are gradually being alleviated, as it has been doing aggressive buybacks.

TAL was previously a Hold-rated name. The prior April 21, 2025 article outlined my mixed view of its expected Q4FY2025 showing and the US-China trade war effects.

Anticipating A Positive Surprise For 2Q

The firm is revealing its latest financials closer to end-Oct.

I think that the sell-side is too pessimistic about TAL.

The consensus 2QFY26 (June 1-Aug 31, 2025) sales and normalized EPS growth rates for the company are +33.8% and +33.9%, respectively on YoY terms.

It had done much better in the preceding quarter.

TAL's turnover rose 38.8% year-on-year to $0.58B in 1QFY2026. During the same period, its bottom-line was 40.0% higher at $0.07.

My take is that it will deliver above-consensus results in two weeks' time.

A leading indicator of future performance is "deferred revenue."

TAL explained in its 20-F that the group's "learning fee" is "collected in advance and is initially recorded as deferred revenue" before being "recognized proportionately as the learning sessions are delivered."

The enterprise's "deferred revenue" jumped +52% YoY in 1QFY26. This has a favorable read-across for the current fiscal year's financial outlook.

Separately, its education-related devices have been selling well.

Goldman Sachs (GS) issued a research note (inaccessible in public) titled "China Education Aug-25 Tracker" on Sep 15. TAL's "Gross Merchandise Value" or "GMV" increase for "learning tablets" improved from 1QFY2026's 65% to 2QFY26's 79% based on GS's evaluation of e-commerce portal data.

I'm forecasting that its Q2 top-line goes up by an above-expectations +39%, taking into account the encouraging numbers presented above.

Also, my predictions point to TAL's normalized EBIT-to-sales metric widening from 10.4% for 2QFY25 to 12.0% in June-Aug this year.

It stressed at the first quarter call in end-July that boosting "overall profitability is one of the company's key priorities." "Operating leverage", "refining our operations at different stages of our workflow", and capitalizing on "today's technology and driving overall efficiency" were cited as TAL's key cost levers according to its results briefing commentary.

I estimate TAL will register a +42% EPS growth for 2QFY2026, thanks to better-than-anticipated revenues and operating margin enhancement.

It's highly probable that the share price will respond favorably to a 2nd quarter beat.

A Richer Valuation Is Warranted For Its Substantial Cash Distributions

TAL is now trading at a modest 1.15x next twelve months' "Enterprise Value-to-Sales" or "EV/S." It also boasts an undemanding ex-cash FY27 P/E of 9.3x. These figures were taken from S&P Capital IQ.

My opinion is that it hasn't been sufficiently rewarded by investors for its shareholder-friendly capital allocation moves.

TAL bought $0.48B worth of its own shares in the Apr-July '25 time frame. This represents a meaningful 7% of its market cap. It is non-dividend paying.

It had net cash amounting to $3.1B on its books as of end-1QFY26. The group has shown an intent to tackle the "lazy balance sheet" issue with the return of excess capital.

TAL's new one-year $0.6B repurchase program was initiated in late-July. That translates into a potential buyback yield of 9%.

Its peer New Oriental Education (EDU) is valued by the market at a comparable forward EV/S of 1.09x.

EDU's net-cash stands at a significant $3.8B (source: S&P Capital IQ). But its expected FY26 (YE May) cash distribution is less than $300M based on its plan to share "50% of the company's net income" with its stockholders. This is below its actual FY25's dividends and repurchases of $0.5B.

TAL's buybacks for Q1FY26 alone ($480M) have already exceeded what EDU is anticipated to pay out in the full year.

TAL is deserving of a valuation premium, given that the company's shareholder return approach has been superior than its peer.

I think that stock can rise to 1.5x EV/S, which is pegged to its three-year historical mean.

You Should Watch These Risks

A 2QFY26 miss could lead to a valuation de-rating and stock price correction for TAL. This can occur in the event of a surge in expenses or slower-than-expected revenue recognition.

The firm might take a pause on buying back its own stock, if it focuses on other cash allocation alternatives such as M&A. Yield-focused investors are likely to be less interested in TAL under that scenario.

My Conclusion

TAL's rating is lifted from a Hold to a Buy.

I see above-consensus Q2 numbers and continued repurchases as re-rating triggers.

Its EV/S multiple can potentially expand by +30% (15/1.15) with my target of 1.5 times mentioned above.

data.title: Alpha Tau Medical: Localized Alpha Radiation, A New Paradigm In Oncology
data.description:
Intro

Alpha Tau Medical (NASDAQ:DRTS) is pioneering the first and (as far as we are aware) only way of delivering alpha particle radiation locally to solid tumors. While the company is not currently well known, this represents a potentially significant breakthrough in oncology given that alpha radiation—which is significantly more effective at killing tumor cells than the beta, gamma, and X-ray radiation used in external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) and currently-approved radiopharmaceuticals—has historically been limited by its poor tissue penetrance and the risk of off-target damage.

The company’s device and procedure, Alpha DaRT, involves the insertion of “sources” (stainless steel or titanium needles) that are coated with Radium-224 directly into the tumor. As the Radium-224 decays, it recoils off of the source, emitting alpha particles and penetrating deeper into tissue as it continues down its six-leg decay chain. This allows for 500-1,000x enhanced tissue penetration of the alpha radiation (vs. its natural penetrance), which so far has enabled the beginnings of what appear to be best-in-class efficacy (and safety) in multiple solid tumor types.

This efficacy, though scattered across a number of different trials (which in some cases are difficult to follow), includes:

  • 100% complete response (CR) rate in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC)

  • 100% objective response rate (ORR) and 50% CR rate in HNSCC in combo with pembro

  • 97% disease control rate (DCR), 19% ORR, and at least one CR in pancreatic cancer

(Note: Some of these data are from very small sample sizes and/or exclusion of some patients)

We discuss these results in further detail below, including a comparison to SoC and leading clinical programs, but suffice it to say that these early data are very encouraging.

While cSCC is technically Alpha Tau’s lead indication with an expected pivotal readout of its US trial mid-2026, we (and we think investors taking an interest in the story) are becoming especially interested in pancreatic cancer, an extremely underserved and lethal cancer, where we note that the data reported to date, which is already more than doubling survival vs. SoC in some patient cohorts, employed only a partial application of Alpha DaRT onto the tumor—i.e. only 20-55% of tumors were covered with Alpha DaRT.With the 30-patient US pancreatic trial enrolling its first patient in early September and a topline readout expected in 3Q26, we think the results could turn out to be even better than the already impressive data to date.

Beyond these primary indications, and with significant support from investigators both in its homeland Israel and internationally, Alpha DaRT is currently being trialed in a number of different cancers. Notably, Alpha DaRT is expected to enroll its first patient in a trial in glioblastoma multiforme (GBM), for which it has Breakthrough Device Designation, in the US by year end.

(Note: some of the timelines shown are out-of-date because this is an older slide deck—the company simplified the newer slide deck to only include its four leading indications)

Because the Alpha Tau story can be a bit complicated, and in the interest of presenting the story as clearly as possible, here are the key points in the Alpha Tau thesis:

  • Alpha DaRT represents the first localized, tumor-agnostic application of alpha particle-emitting radiation, which is far more effective than beta/gamma/X-ray radiation.

  • The only other viable application of alpha radiation to date has been radiopharmaceuticals, which are highly localized to cells expressing specific druggable target proteins, allowing for minimal off-target effects but also limiting application to a handful of cancer indications (e.g. prostate, GEP-NETs). These are still clinical-stage.

  • As a tumor-agnostic therapy, Alpha DaRT is currently being (or will imminently be) trialed in eight cancer types. The company has proven its ability to conduct multiple trials and has enrolled 100+ patients to date internationally.

  • Lead indication cSCC, which is already approved in Israel (but not commercialized), represents a proof-of-concept in a relatively non-lethal cancer, with a 100% CR and a pivotal top-line readout from US trial expected in mid-2026.

  • HNSCC (also already approved in Israel) has reported impressive data in combo with pembro, better than leading clinical-stage therapies, but is currently in a holding pattern as the company decides whether to start an expensive US checkpoint inhibitor (CPI) combination trial on its own or wait for a partner. Also currently awaiting a PMDA approval decision in Japan for HNSCC (expected by year end).

  • Pancreatic cancer, likely its most exciting program currently, has reported significantly better data vs. SoC despite employing only 20-55% tumor coverage, suggesting topline data from its 30-patient US trial expected in 3Q26 could show even greater efficacy.

  • GBM is expected to enroll its first patient in a 10-patient US trial before year end.

  • Multiple cases of shrinkage and resolution of distal/metastatic cancer lesions suggest Alpha DaRT’s activation of a systemic immune response.

  • Alpha DaRT’s extremely mild side effect profile should allow for combination therapies across indications.

  • Only burning ~$6 million per quarter; should have $75+ million of cash as of 9/30/25.

Despite these early indications of Alpha DaRT being a potential gamechanger in oncology and a broad clinical pipeline across numerous indications, Alpha Tau is currently trading at only ~$340 million of market capitalization.

Aside from being clearly undervalued on a simple future discounted cashflow basis (assuming approvals and eventual commercialization in multiple indications), Alpha Tau’s valuation looks especially low in light of recent business development and trading activity, including:

  1. A flurry of acquisitions in late 2023 to 2024 of alpha radiopharmaceuticals companies, including Eli Lilly’s acquisition of POINT Biopharma for $1.4 billion in October 2023, BMS’s acquisition of RayzeBio for $4.1 billion in December 2023, and AstraZeneca’s acquisition of Fusion Pharmaceuticals for $2.4 billion in June 2024. All of these radiopharmaceuticals companies are currently going after the same few cancer types.

  1. Nanobiotix (NBTX): Provides a “radioenhancer” that makes external radiation therapy more effective. Still has all the downsides of external radiation (e.g. toxicity, healthy tissue damage) and has reported lower efficacy than Alpha DaRT’s early data in their overlapping indications (primarily HNSCC). Currently trading at $1.3 billion of market cap after a recent rallyfueled by data updates.

  1. Merus (MRUS): Recently announced it will be acquired by Genmab for $8 billion, primarily for its HNSCC program which reported impressive data earlier in the year.

Alpha Tau appears to be entering a critical period during which it will go from being (or being perceived as) a small, relatively unknown, mostly Israeli-based oncology biotech, to a unique, paradigm-changing oncology company with multiple ongoing, potentially high-profile US trials. While it has taken some time for the story to come together, we think the next 12-24 months will be transformative for Alpha Tau and expect a concordant re-rating of its valuation during that time period.

Alpha DaRT

Alpha DaRT (Diffusing α-emitters Radiation Therapy) involves the injection of thin metallic seeds (tiny metal rods made of either steel or titanium) that have been coated with Radium-224 directly into tumors. After implantation, short-lived daughters of the Radium-224, such as Radon-220, Polonium-216, and Lead-212 recoil off of the metal seed and deeper into the tissue, suffusing the tumour with high-linear energy transfer (LET) alpha particles, while sparing most of the surrounding healthy tissue. The seeds are either removed after 3-4 weeks or, in some cases, are left inside the body.

One of Alpha DaRT’s key innovations is the way that Alpha Tau traps the Radium-224 on the surface of the metallic seed, which allows the daughter particles to recoil off of the source/seed, allowing the isotopes to penetrate 4-5 mm into tissue, which is roughly 500-1,000x further than the ~40-90 microns that alpha-emitting isotopes normally penetrate through human tissue. The limited tissue penetrance of alpha particles has been one of the primary impediments to their utilization in radiation therapy, and why targeted alpha radiopharmaceuticals have been the first successful application of alpha particles (because they deliver the radioisotope directly to the desired cells).

This increased tissue penetrance allows Alpha DaRT unlocks the superior efficacy of alpha radiation, which, due to alpha particles’ greater mass and higher energy transfer rate, causes double-stranded DNA breaks, which potently trigger cell death. Beta radiation, by comparison, primarily causes single-stranded DNA breaks, which the cell can often repair.

In addition to enhanced damage through double stranded DNA breaks, there are a number of complementary mechanisms by which Alpha DaRT’s unlocking of alpha radiation creates such powerful efficacy and safety advantages over existing treatments. CMO Dr. Robert Den summarized these advantages in this video from early July:

  1. Alpha DaRT’s localized application, limited tissue penetrance compared to other forms of radiation, and the highly potent nature of alpha radiation allows Alpha DaRT to administer a significantly higher level of radiation than what can be achieved with other radiotherapeutic modalities. In essence, EBRT (and other radiation therapies) are dose-limited by toxicity, while Alpha DaRT essentially removes this limitation.

  2. The company has have found that the alpha particles don’t diffuse nearly as much through healthy tissue as they do through cancerous tissue (because of the disorganized nature of cancerous tissue), which further limits the damage caused to surrounding tissue.

  3. Because DaRT is administered directly inside the tumor and spares healthy tissue, it avoids killing the T cell and B cell suites that are usually sitting around the periphery of the tumor, which are important for killing the tumor cells.

  4. The cell destruction caused by alpha radiation activates the surrounding immune cells, creating immune activation towards tumors, i.e. making a cold tumor hot.

  5. The recognition of these leaked tumor-specific proteins and markers by the immune system leads to a systemic response that allows the immune system to detect and defeat distant lesions, called an abscopal effect.

Alpha DaRT’s true innovation is allowing for intratumoral delivery of alpha radiation therapy, which to our knowledge is completely unique in the oncology space.

Pipeline

While Alpha DaRT’s mechanism is applicable to essentially any solid tumor type, the company originally focused on external/superficial tumor types like cSCC and HNSCC due to the practical advantages and the ability to easily monitor improvements. More recently, Alpha Tau began expanding into cancers with severe unmet need like pancreatic and glioblastoma.

The company’s pipeline includes eight indications for which it has either completed, begun, or is about to begin trials (some timelines in the below image are out-of-date).

In total, the company has 24 trials that are either actively recruiting or expected to begin recruiting soon according to ClinicalTrials.gov, many of which are investigator-initiated.

Skin Cancer

In its most advanced indication, cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), Alpha DaRT is actually already approved in Israel (approved in 2020), though the company has not yet launched commercially as it is focusing its financial and operational resources on the US (where it will be able to set a higher global pricing precedent). While cSCC is less lethal than most cancers, the most common treatments like surgical resection and EBRT can be deformative and/or come with significant side effects.

The data Alpha DaRT has reported in cSCC to date, its largest in any indication, suggest it could provide an essentially curative treatment option with comparatively limited disfigurement and side effects.

The company is currently conducting a pivotal study in the US that it expects will readout data around 2Q26/mid-2026, with a submission for approval expected thereafter, though we note that the company may delay commercialization. We also note that Alpha DaRT was awarded Breakthrough Therapy Designation in June 2021 in cSCC.

Data To Date

28-Patient First-In-Human Trial

The first trial of Alpha DaRT in skin (and oral) SCC enrolled 28 patients who either failed or were unfit to receive definitive therapy (resection/EBRT). The trial reported:

  • 100% ORR at six weeks post treatment

  • Including a 79% CR rate.

These results were enough to support the approval of Alpha DaRT for skin SCC in Israel.

71-Patient PooledAnalysis

This pooled analysis spanned 81 tumors in 71 patients across patients with recurrent or unresectable cSCC, HNSCC, or basal cell carcinoma (BCC). As stated above, it includes the 28-patient initial pilot study we just discussed. Across the 71 patients, Alpha DaRT delivered:

  • 99% ORR

  • Including an 89% CR rate

The two-year actuarial local recurrence-free survival (essentially two-year PFS) was 77% (shown below), and if you look at the recurrence/survival plot below, you can also see that the 4-year PFS is around 65%. These results would have been even better if you were to exclude the 28-patient pilot trial, which had lower efficacy and durability than subsequent trials as the company initially prioritized safety and optimized the Alpha DaRT device/procedure.

As for safety, 27% of patients developed acute grade 2 or higher toxicities, all of which were resolved with conservative measures.

10-Patient US Pilot Trial

Memorial Sloan Kettering led a US pilot trial of 10 patients with skin SCC that was published in JAMA. These patients had either failed prior radiation therapy (60%) or had a tumor that was deemed unresectable (40%).

  • All 10 patients (100%) achieved complete response.

It is worth noting that all of these results were achieved without combination with immunotherapy or chemotherapy, some combination of which would likely be utilized for at least some patients in a real-world setting.

Market and Treatment Landscape

Despite relatively high response rates seen with surgical resection (e.g. Mohs; used in the majority of patients), non-surgical local treatments (e.g. cryotherapy, photodynamic therapy; used in very early-stage disease), and EBRT (used alone and in combination with surgery or therapeutics), a fair number of patients are either ineligible for or fail these treatments. Alpha Tau will target the approximately 64k patients in the US that have tumor recurrence after surgery or EBRT and patients with local metastasis.

This cohort of “advanced” cSCC patients is significantly underserved by existing therapies. Treatment options primarily consist of checkpoint inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and chemotherapy.

Checkpoint inhibitors, the most efficacious (and durable) treatment for these patients, report:

  • ORRs ranging from 45-70% (Zhang et al., 2022; Hein et al. 2023)

  • CR rates ranging from 7-28% (Zhang et al., 2022; Hein et al. 2023)

Alpha DaRT so far appears to represent a dramatic improvement over current SoC efficacy in these patients.

Pivotal US Skin SCC Trial (ReSTART)

The company is currently conducting a pivotal trial in 86 patients with recurrent cSCC for which it expects to complete enrollment of ReSTART in 4Q25 (likely towards the latter half; previous guidance was 3Q25), with topline results expected in 2Q26/mid-2026. The company expects to submit for approval with the FDA shortly thereafter, which could support potential commercialization in 2027.

It is also worth mentioning that Alpha DaRT is also in two other cSCC trials:

  1. An 80-patient investigator-initiated study in France in different malignant cutaneous tumors (mostly SCC and BCC) which enrolled its first patient in 2022.

  2. An investigator-initiated study led by Emory University in immunocompromised cSCC patients is expected to begin enrolling in the 2H25

While cSCC is Alpha Tau’s most advanced indication, we note that the company may not elect to commercialize in cSCC after receiving approval, primarily because cSCC is a relatively non-lethal cancer with good treatment options, which could require more resources to sell into. We think it is likely that the company could choose to prioritize its resources for advancement in pancreatic cancer,and to a lesser extent GBM.

Regardless of whether the company decides to pursue commercialization immediately upon approval or to wait, the cSCC data are a nice, visible, large(ish) sample size illustration of Alpha DaRT’s powerful potential on both efficacy and safety. We still expect the ReSTART readout in mid-2026 to be one of the larger near/medium-term catalysts for Alpha Tau.

Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (HNSCC)

The company is also advancing Alpha DaRT in HNSCC, which is similar to cSCC (both affect squamous cells), with the distinction that HNSCC affects mucosal cells of the head and neck region, while cSCC affects epidermal cells in the skin. HNSCC is generally harder to treat, with lower rates of tumors eligible for resection, lower response rates to immunotherapy, and significantly higher rates of mortality, especially in the recurrent/metastatic setting.

In HNSCC, Alpha DaRT:

  • Has approval for oral cavity SCC (which encompasses most HNSCC cases) in Israel

  • Is awaiting a decision on its approval application in Japan (expected 2H25)

  • Reported early data from a Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) combination trial in January

While the data is much more limited than for cSCC, early indications again point to Alpha DaRT having potentially best-in-class efficacy when used in combination with a checkpoint inhibitor (current SoC).

11-Patient Pilot Study

These 11 patients had all received either prior external beam radiation or brachytherapy (i.e. had recurrent disease). The trial administered DaRT as monotherapy (no checkpoint inhibitor) and reported:

  • 82% ORR

  • Including a 27% CR rate

  • DCR of 91%, meaning only one patient had progressive disease

Pembro Combination

The company also conducted a trial of Alpha DaRT in combination with pembrolizumab in Israel. This trial includes patients with either metastatic or locally advanced disease.

In January, the company reported interim results from the first 8 patients (6 metastatic; 2 locally advanced) in this trial, showing:

  • 75% ORR

  • Including 38% CR rate

If you exclude the two patients that died prior to their first evaluation, the results improve to:

  • 100% ORR

  • Including 50% CR rate

The company also provides an example of a patient from the trial that was treated in 2022 and was still alive as of October 2024 follow up with no evidence of disease.

While the sample size is quite small and we will await an update on the durability of these responses, the early efficacy signals are promising. Unfortunately, though this trial was designed to enroll up to 48 patients, an interim analysis determined that the trial had already been successful in demonstrating Alpha DaRT’s proof of concept and enrollment was stopped.

As such, the company is in a bit of a holding period with regards to HNSCC, considering whether it should embark on an expensive checkpoint inhibitor combination trial in the US on its own or wait until it can potentially strike up a partnership with a larger checkpoint inhibitor pharma company. In the meantime, the approval decision expected from the Japanese PMDA before the end of the year could serve as an incremental catalyst for the stock, though we believe the company will likely continue to wait for US approval to commercialize anything.

Market and SoC

Like most companies developing drugs for HNSCC, Alpha Tau is focusing on recurrent unresectable and metastatic patients (r/m HNSCC). The company estimates there are roughly 15k cases of R/M HNSCC per year in the US.

This is the same patient population that was investigated in pembrolizumab’s pivotal KEYNOTE-048 study that led to its approval in r/m HNSCC, where it currently serves as first-line standard of care treatment.

In the pivotal KEYNOTE-048 trial, pembrolizumab reported:

  • 17-23% ORR

  • Including 5-8% CR

  • mOS of 14.9 months

The company believes that these pembrolizumab results, which so far Alpha DaRT appears to very handily beat, will serve as the benchmark to beat when it decides to initiate another study in HNSCC.

Clinical Development Landscape

With pembrolizumab and other available treatments leaving a high unmet need for patients, there are a number of companies advancing potential treatments in HNSCC, most of which are conducting trials in combination with pembrolizumab. Bicara Therapeutics and Merus are two of the most prominent in the space.

Bicara Therapeutics

Bicara is advancing ficerafusp alfa, a bifunctional antibody targeting EGFR and TGF-β. The company provided extended follow-up data from its 28-patient pembrolizumab combination Phase 1 trial at ASCO in June that showed:

  • 54% ORR

  • Including 21% CR rate

  • mPFS 9.9 months

  • mOS 21.3 months

Bicara began a pivotal Phase 2/3 trial for ficerafusp alfa in combination with pembrolizumab in February.

Merus

Merus, which was just acquired by Genmab for $8 billion, is advancing petosemtamab, a bispecific antibody targeting EGFR and LGR5, both as a monotherapy in second-line R/M HNSCC and in combination with pembrolizumab in first-line R/M HNSCC. Petosemtamab has Breakthrough Therapy Designation in both indications.

In May, Merus reported updated efficacy data in combination with pembrolizumab in 45 PD-L1-positive R/M HNSCC patients showing:

  • 63% ORR

  • Including 14% CR rate

  • mPFS of 9 months

  • mOS not reached; 79% at 12 months

While Merus is also pursuing colorectal and some other solid cancers, this HNSCC data was the primary driver of the recent acquisition by Genmab for $8 billion.This highlights the potential value of Alpha Tau in HNSCC alone given it has so far delivered better results than both Merus and Bicara, though data is still very early. We also note that Alpha DaRT, as a local and relatively safe therapy, could theoretically be used in combination with immunotherapies like Merus’s petosemtamab.

We would expect more clarity from the company regarding next steps in HNSCC in the coming months, as well as the potential for updated data from the now-halted Israeli trial.

Pancreatic Cancer

Pancreatic cancer seems to have become the most exciting indication for Alpha DaRT in 2025. Pancreatic cancer is the third-leading cause of cancer deaths in the US, with about 50% of cases already metastatic upon diagnosis (Stage IV) and up to 87% of patients have tumors that are not surgically resectable, either because of poor margins or metastatic disease. There are very few treatments available for unresectable and metastatic patients, essentially consisting solely of chemotherapy, with median survival of 5-8 months.

To adapt the Alpha DaRT treatment to an internal organ such as the pancreas, Alpha Tau piggybacked on an endoscopic diagnostic procedure that is already performed in pancreatic cancer patients called endoscopic fine needle aspiration (FNA). Alpha Tau created a device that allows for the loading of Alpha DaRT sources into the needle that is used during FNA, which allows the Alpha DaRT sources to be applied directly to the pancreatic tumor via ultrasound (video here).

In this interview, CMO Dr. Robert Den explains that this application of Alpha DaRT requires very little training for advanced endoscopists (an endoscopists specialized in diagnostic and therapeutic procedures). The procedure takes about 45 minutes once a physician is proficient, and patients are discharged from the hospital on the same day. During the January R&D day, Dr. Corey Miller, advanced endoscopist and assistant professor at McGill University, also testified to the ease of the procedure.

Data to Date

During the January R&D day, the company presented data from 41 patients that were enrolled across three different ongoing trials being conducted in Canada and Israel.

As you can see below, the trials included a heterogenous group of patients, with the large majority of patients having metastatic disease and having received at least one line of prior chemotherapy.

As of the January cutoff, data was available from 33 of the 41 patients (5 had not yet had a follow-up and 3 discontinued the trial), which reported:

  • 91% disease control rate (DCR)

  • 18% ORR

When excluding the first two patients treated who received only a very small dose of Alpha DaRT, the efficacy improved to:

  • 97% disease control

  • 19% ORR

So, excluding the two low-dose patients, only 1 out of the 33 patients had progressive disease as their best response per RECIST criteria.

Importantly, Alpha DaRT has also proven to be relatively safe in pancreatic cancer patients, in stark contrast to existing treatment, with zero Grade 4 adverse events, and, notably, zero cases of pancreatitis reported. This speaks to Alpha DaRT’s minimal diffusion into healthy tissue and its potential to be used in combination with other therapies.

The company also provided a case study from its first CR in pancreatic cancer, in which a patient had both a complete response in the primary pancreatic tumor as well as resolution of liver metastasis.

While these data are already impressive in the context of what is currently available in pancreatic cancer (discussed below), it is important to note that all of these patients were treated with only ~20-55% tumor coverage (the proportion of the primary tumor covered by Alpha DaRT sources) according to the company’s R&D day. The partial coverage was done out of an abundance of caution and as the company and physicians became more comfortable with the use of Alpha DaRT in pancreatic cancer, and suggests that the US trial could report further improved efficacy.

Putting the Data in Context

The company also provided a segmented analysis of the heterogenous patient population to make it easier to put the results into context for different patients groups.

In patients with newly diagnosed pancreatic cancer that were not eligible for (or refusing) chemotherapy, Alpha DaRT delivered 7.5 months median overall survival (MOS) versus 3-3.5 months for standard of care:

In Stage IV metastatic patients that had received and progressed on first-line FOLFIRINOX (a combination of five chemotherapy agents), Alpha DaRT had extended mOS to at least 15.1 months as of the data cutoff (8 of 10 patients were still alive) versus 10-11 months mOS for FOLFIRINOX treatment. (Note: a small number of these patients continued receiving FOLFIRINOX concurrently with Alpha DaRT).

In patients that had failed second-line gemcitabine-piclataxel (GnP), Alpha DaRT extended mOS to 23 months versus the 7.5-10 months of mOS seen with GnP. These patients also had a mOS of 9 months from the time of receiving Alpha DaRT, which means that third-line patients with essentially no other treatment options had a 9 month mOS on Alpha DaRT monotherapy.

While the data are a bit messy on account of being accumulated across various different proof-of-concept trials (including one basket trial), the early indications point to a potential for Alpha DaRT to provide significant advantages over standard of care, potentially more than doubling patient survival in some settings, with room for further improvement as tumors are treated with greater coverage.

We expect an update on this pancreatic data in the next few months, potentially in another January R&D day, where we should see longer duration data and some additional patients.

US Trial

In early September, Alpha Tau announced that it treated the first patient in its 30-patient US trial. This trial is set to enroll 15 newly diagnosed metastatic and 15 newly diagnosed locally-advanced pancreatic patients and will administer Alpha DaRT during a course of modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX).

The principal investigators on the trial are Dr. Mark D’Andrea and Dr. Isaac Raijman of the University Cancer Center in Houston, Texas, and the trial is enrolling at 16 hospitals, including Emory University, NYU Langone, Montefiore Medical Center.

The company plans to complete enrollment of the trial in the 1Q26, with topline results expected in 3Q25 (roughly). While patients will likely have to be monitored for longer periods to show mOS superiority vs. standard of care, the company should be able to report response and early mPFS data in 3Q26.

Market and Treatment Landscape

There are about 66k newly diagnosed cases of pancreatic cancer each year in the US, up to 87% of which are non-resectable either due to metastatic disease (~50% of newly diagnosed patients) or poor margins.

As was shown above, the two primary treatments for these patients, FOLFIRINOX and gemcitabine + piclataxel (GnP), are fairly very ineffective, extending survival by just a few months. However, because there are so few treatment options, the global piclataxel market, for example, is estimated at around $6-7 billion of sales annually, and BMS’s Abraxane (piclataxel), even after being generic for 20 years, did $875 million of worldwide sales in 2024 (link).

While there are a number of companies pursuing pancreatic cancer there are a few companies with particularly interesting programs we will highlight.

Immuneering

Immuneering’s MEK inhibitor atebimetinib has reported impressive Phase 2a data in combination with GnP in first-line pancreatic cancer patients, showing 86% OS at 9 months versus 47% for GnP alone.

Immuneering also reported impressive response data, including 81% DCR, 39% ORR, and even a CR.

In addition to the CR, Immuneering has also reported that atebimetinib delivered the resolution of a distal bone lesion in a third-line patient in its Phase 1 trial.

Especially for a systemic therapy, these data are quite impressive, with significant improvement versus SoC and a DCR approaching that seen in the Alpha DaRT data to date, though it is important to note that the patient population and treatment settings are not directly comparable.

Actuate Therapeutics

In May, Actuate Therapeutics reported Phase 2 data for its GSK-3β inhibitor elraglusib that showed mOS of 10.1 months for elraglusib + GnP versus 7.2 months for GnP in first-line metastatic pancreatic cancer. Additionally, it reported 44% OS at 12 months versus 22% for GnP alone.

While these data represent an improvement over standard of care, they weren’t quite as impressive as Actuate’s earlier Phase 2 trial which showed a 15.3 month mOS and actually reported two CRs.

Both Immuneering and Actuate have reported impressive data in pancreatic cancer and will be interesting to monitor going forward. While the results (particularly from Immuneering) look somewhat comparable to Alpha DaRT’s data in some respects, we would note that the existing data set for Alpha DaRT makes comparisons difficult—Alpha DaRT was being used as a monotherapy in the large majority of patients, was being trialed in later lines of therapy, and as we have said, was using only partial tumor coverage. It will be interesting to see the much cleaner, more interpretable results from Alpha DaRT’s ongoing US trial expected in 3Q26.

Regardless of the fight for superior efficacy, however, Alpha DaRT’s uniqueness as a local treatment with very minimal side effects and potential systemic immune-stimulating effects should make it a key addition to the essentially barren pancreatic cancer treatment landscape, as well as a potentially valuable combination therapy.

Other Indications

Alpha Tau is also set to dose its first patient in a 10-patient proof-of-concept trial in glioblastoma multiforme (GBM). The company developed an intricate delivery device that is able to apply Alpha DaRT directly to tumors in the brain without causing significant damage to brain matter. Alpha DaRT was awarded Breakthrough Device Designation in 2021. The 10-patient study is being led by Ohio State University and is expected to begin enrollment sometime before the end of the year.

Outside of its lead indications, Alpha DaRT is also being trialed in liver, lung, prostate, and vulvar cancer in small pilot studies.

Path Forward and Catalyst Overview

To summarize, Alpha Tau’s should have a rather busy catalyst calendar over the next 12+ months:

Cutaneous SCC

  • Expects to complete enrollment in potentially pivotal trial around YE25

  • Data from 86-patient trial expected around mid-2026

  • May elect to delay commercial launch to prioritize pancreatic and other higher-impact indications

HNSCC

  • Awaiting determination from Japanese PMDA on approval in HNSCC in Japan; expected 2H25

  • Potential update on the patients that were enrolled in the pembro combination trial prior to halting (e.g. further durability data and potential additional patients)

Pancreatic

  • Additional data from ongoing Israeli/Canadian trials (additional patients and durability data) could be presented in coming months

GBM
